tldr: At the headline layer this story is locked into a single dominant frame: China's year-long orbital mission as capability preparation for a 2030 Moon landing. France24 is the only outlet to use race language ('la course vers la Lune'); all other titles use mission-planning language ('targets,' 'eyes,' 'ambitions'). Two of three Chinese-outlet headlines cover BCI standards and AI development rather than the launch, making the china bucket a structural outlier at 0.457 cosine similarity. Hong Kong Free Press uniquely foregrounds the astronaut's rank as 'a police superintendent' — a detail absent from every other outlet's headline.

frames.sub_frame: Year-long orbital stay as 2030 Moon prep

evidence.quote: l'un d'eux restera un an dans la station Tiangong
China launches year-long orbital mission with lunar ambitions
Chinesischer Raumfahrer bleibt erstmals ein Jahr im All
1 of 3 astronauts set for yearlong stay
year-long space mission as it eyes 2030 moon landing
crewed space flight as part of Moon ambitions
China schedules next moonshot this year

frames.sub_frame: China racing or targeting 2030 Moon landing

evidence.quote: la Chine dans la course vers la Lune

frames.sub_frame: First HK astronaut: former police superintendent

evidence.quote: with Hong Kong astronaut – a police superintendent – aboard

single_outlet_findings.finding: Only outlet to use 'race' language in the headline ('la course vers la Lune'); every other outlet uses 'targets,' 'eyes,' 'ambitions,' or 'goals' — neutral mission-planning vocabulary.

Only outlet to headline the astronaut's institutional rank as 'a police superintendent' rather than the generic 'police officer,' foregrounding a precise title that carries institutional weight in the post-2020 Hong Kong security context.

Only article in the cluster covering not the Shenzhou-23 launch but BCI national standards — grouped here by topic proximity to China tech governance, not the space mission; one of two china-bucket headlines entirely absent from the launch story.

bottom_line: Headline coverage of Shenzhou-23 is overwhelmingly a capability story — the year-long orbital stay as a stepping stone to the 2030 Moon landing — with France24 the lone outlier naming this a race, and Hong Kong Free Press the lone outlier placing the astronaut's police rank, not just her city, in the headline.
